modules.title: Frame of reference, scalars & vectors
modules.html:
A frame of reference is a system relative to which motion is described. Motion is always relative — a passenger in a bus is at rest relative to the bus but moves relative to the road.

Scalar vs vector

  • Scalar: magnitude only. Examples: distance, speed, mass, time, energy, temperature.
  • Vector: magnitude and direction. Examples: displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum, weight.

modules.title: Speed, velocity, acceleration
modules.html:
Average speed = total distance / total time.
Velocity = displacement / time (vector).
Acceleration = (final velocity − initial velocity) / time.

Uniform velocity: equal displacements in equal times, direction unchanged. Uniform acceleration: equal changes in velocity in equal times.

examples.q: A car goes 100 m in 5 s. Speed?
Velocity changes from 4 m/s to 20 m/s in 4 s. Acceleration?

modules.title: Motion graphs
modules.html:
On a distance–time graph the slope gives speed. A straight line = uniform speed; a curve = changing speed.

On a velocity–time graph the slope gives acceleration and the area under the graph gives displacement.

  • Horizontal line ⇒ constant velocity, zero acceleration.
  • Straight line rising ⇒ uniform acceleration.
  • Straight line falling ⇒ uniform deceleration.

examples.q: Area under a v–t graph represents…
Slope of a distance–time graph represents…

modules.title: Equations of uniformly accelerated motion
modules.html:
For constant acceleration:

  1. v = u + at
  2. s = ut + ½at²
  3. v² = u² + 2as
  4. s = ½(u + v)t

Where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, a = acceleration, s = displacement, t = time.

examples.q: Car accelerates from rest at 2 m/s² for 5 s. Distance?
u = 10 m/s, a = 2 m/s², t = 4 s. v?

modules.title: Motion under gravity
modules.html:
Near the Earth's surface, free-fall acceleration g ≈ 10 m/s² (or 9.8 m/s²) downwards.

  • Dropped object: u = 0, a = g, v = gt, h = ½gt².
  • Projected upward: a = −g. At maximum height v = 0, so u = gt and h = u²/2g.
  • Time to rise = time to fall (symmetry).

examples.q: Stone dropped for 3 s. Speed on impact? (g = 10)
Ball thrown up at 20 m/s. Max height?

quiz.q: On a d–t graph, a horizontal line means
On a v–t graph, a horizontal line means
options: zero acceleration exists but body may be moving
quiz.solution: Slope is zero but velocity is non-zero; body moves with constant velocity — both (0) and (3) apply. Best answer: constant velocity.
